content.text: The gap between a successful discovery workshop and a sprint-ready product backlog is where many AI feature delivery efforts lose fidelity. Discovery-to-backlog handoff criteria define the explicit conditions that must be satisfied before a workshop's outputs are accepted as implementable backlog items — preventing teams from beginning development on an incomplete or ambiguous foundation.
What It Is

Discovery-to-backlog handoff criteria are a set of verifiable conditions that act as a quality gate between the output of a feature discovery workshop and the backlog items that development teams will implement. The criteria specify what must be true about the workshop output — what artefacts must exist, what questions must be answered, what risks must be owned — before product management is permitted to create stories and schedule them for implementation.

For generative AI feature development, the handoff criteria extend beyond standard Agile definition-of-ready requirements to include AI-specific preconditions: model behaviour constraints documented, LLM output acceptance criteria defined, non-deterministic failure modes identified and owned, and safety evaluation criteria established. Without these extensions, teams routinely begin building AI features before the requirements for how the AI should behave in edge cases are understood.

The criteria function as a contract between the discovery function (product management, UX, business analysis) and the implementation function (engineering, data science, platform). Both parties agree that when the criteria are met, the backlog is ready for sprint planning. When any criterion is unmet, the discovery phase is not complete and additional workshop sessions, spikes, or stakeholder decisions are required before handoff.
Why It Matters

Without explicit handoff criteria, the pressure to begin implementation drives teams to start building before discovery is actually complete. The most common consequence for AI features is that engineers make implicit assumptions about how the model should behave in ambiguous cases — assumptions that diverge from what product management and users expect. These divergences are expensive to discover in QA and extremely expensive to discover in production.

A second consequence of missing handoff criteria is what practitioners call "scope seep from discovery" — the gradual widening of feature scope during implementation as engineers discover cases that were not addressed in the discovery session. Without documented handoff criteria, there is no clear signal that a discovered edge case requires a new discovery session rather than an engineering decision, and individual engineers end up making product decisions unilaterally.
Benefits
Prevents premature implementation
— the gate provides a shared, enforceable signal that the feature is not yet ready to build, preventing the discovery-to-build transition from being driven by schedule pressure rather than readiness.
Forces resolution of ambiguity
— criteria that require all hotspots to be owned before handoff cannot be gamed by adding more hotspot stickers without assigning them; the gate quantifies incompleteness.
Establishes traceability
— because handoff criteria require that each backlog item links to a source discovery artefact, the chain from user need to implementation decision is always auditable.
Creates AI-specific safety checkpoints
— requiring safety evaluation criteria before handoff ensures that safety is a discovery-phase concern, not an afterthought discovered during testing.
Reduces mid-sprint scope changes
— features that pass the handoff gate are better specified, which reduces the frequency of incomplete stories that surface their missing requirements during development.
Trade-offs and Limitations

Handoff criteria can become a bureaucratic blocker if applied rigidly to small features or spike investigations that do not warrant full discovery workshop treatment — judgment is required about which deliverables require a full gate.

AI behaviour requirements are inherently probabilistic — criteria that require complete specification of model outputs may be impossible to fully satisfy before some degree of experimentation, creating tension between the gate and iterative AI development practices.

The criteria must be maintained as the organisation's AI capabilities mature — criteria appropriate for a team's first LLM feature may become too lenient after the team has shipped several features and understands the common failure modes.

Handoff criteria are only effective if both the discovery team and the development team have agreed on them and hold each other accountable — criteria imposed by one team on another without shared buy-in will be worked around.
How It Works

The handoff process consists of three steps: gate evaluation, conversion, and readiness confirmation. Gate evaluation checks the discovery output against the criteria. Conversion transforms the discovery artefacts into backlog items using defined mapping rules. Readiness confirmation is a structured review meeting between product management and engineering that approves the backlog for sprint planning.
Standard Handoff Criteria
The following criteria apply to all feature handoffs. Each must be verifiably true — not estimated or partially true — before the backlog conversion step begins.
All discovery hotspots have an assigned owner and a resolution deadline.
All bounded contexts are named and have a list of their key events and aggregates.
The success metrics for the feature are quantified (not "improve user experience" but "95th percentile response time under 2 seconds").
The out-of-scope exclusions are explicitly documented and agreed by both product and engineering.
At least one domain expert has reviewed the bounded context map and confirmed it accurately represents the domain.
AI-Specific Handoff Criteria
For features involving LLMs or agentic workflows, the following additional criteria must be satisfied before handoff:
The target LLM or model family is specified with a fallback defined if the primary model is unavailable.

The data types and fields that may be sent to the LLM are documented, with confirmation that no prohibited data (PII, PHI, confidential business data) will be included without explicit de-identification.
Output acceptance criteria are written in testable form — specifying what a correct, incorrect, and marginal LLM response looks like for the feature's core use cases.
Human-in-the-loop checkpoints are enumerated with the policy basis for each ("required because regulation X mandates human approval for transactions over $Y").
At least three failure mode scenarios are documented with the expected system behaviour for each (graceful degradation, escalation, or hard stop).
The evaluation strategy for the feature is defined — specifying how the team will measure whether the AI component is performing acceptably in production.
Backlog Conversion Mapping

Once the gate is passed, the discovery artefacts are converted to backlog items using a defined mapping. Each bounded context becomes an epic. Each aggregate within a context becomes a component or service story. Each policy or compliance rule becomes an acceptance criterion on the relevant story. Each hotspot becomes a spike task with the assigned owner. Each AI constraint becomes a non-functional requirement (NFR) story. Each risk becomes an entry in the feature risk register.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Discovery-to-backlog handoff flow with gate and conversion mapping

content.text: Discovery outputs (purple) pass through a handoff gate (amber) that evaluates against explicit criteria. Gate failures return the feature to the workshop for additional discovery. Gate passes trigger the conversion step (green) that maps each artefact type to its corresponding backlog item type, producing a ready backlog (blue) suitable for sprint planning.
When to Use It

Full handoff criteria evaluation is warranted for any feature that consumed a structured discovery workshop, will require more than one sprint to implement, involves an LLM or agentic workflow, or has compliance, safety, or data privacy implications. For small bug fixes or trivial UI changes, a lightweight version of the criteria (hotspots owned, scope documented) is sufficient without a formal gate review.

The criteria also serve as a retrospective tool: when a feature encounters significant scope change, regression, or safety issue during implementation, reviewing the handoff gate output identifies which criterion was unmet or inaccurate at the time of handoff. This creates a learning loop that improves the criteria over time.
How to Implement
Define the criteria collaboratively

: Product management and engineering leads jointly write the handoff criteria for the organisation. The criteria must be verifiable, not aspirational — "all hotspots have owners" is verifiable; "all risks are mitigated" is not.
Create a gate checklist

: Encode the criteria as a checklist (in Notion, Confluence, or a PR template) that the product manager fills out after each discovery session. The checklist must be reviewed by an engineering representative before the backlog conversion begins.
Build the conversion mapping
: Document the mapping from discovery artefact types to backlog item types. Codify this as a story template for each item type, with required fields that correspond to the discovery artefact source.
Schedule a readiness review

: After conversion, hold a 30-minute backlog readiness review with the product manager, tech lead, and at least one engineer who will implement the feature. The review confirms that stories are unambiguous, estimates are feasible, and all mandatory fields are populated.
Track handoff quality metrics
: Measure the rate of mid-sprint scope changes per feature and the rate of discovery re-opens (features returned to the gate). These metrics reveal whether the criteria are well-calibrated.
Retrospect and update the criteria quarterly

: Review the gate failure reasons and mid-sprint scope change patterns to identify which criteria are too weak (allowing under-specified features through) or too strict (blocking features that are adequately specified for their risk level).
Examples
The following implements the handoff gate as a Python class that evaluates a discovery output against the criteria and produces a structured pass/fail report:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional
from enum import Enum

class GateResult(Enum):
    PASS = "pass"
    FAIL = "fail"
    CONDITIONAL = "conditional"  # passes with minor outstanding items

@dataclass
class CriterionResult:
    name: str
    passed: bool
    evidence: str
    blocking: bool = True  # blocking criteria fail the gate; non-blocking are warnings

@dataclass
class DiscoveryOutput:
    feature_name: str
    bounded_contexts: list[str]
    hotspots: list[dict]       # [{"id": ..., "owner": ..., "deadline": ...}]
    ai_constraints_documented: bool
    failure_modes_count: int
    success_metrics: list[str]
    out_of_scope: list[str]
    llm_model_specified: bool
    human_checkpoints: list[str]
    evaluation_strategy_defined: bool

class HandoffGate:
    def evaluate(self, output: DiscoveryOutput) -> tuple[GateResult, list[CriterionResult]]:
        results: list[CriterionResult] = []

        # --- Standard criteria ---
        unowned = [h for h in output.hotspots if not h.get("owner")]
        results.append(CriterionResult(
            name="All hotspots have owners",
            passed=len(unowned) == 0,
            evidence=f"{len(unowned)} hotspot(s) without owner" if unowned
                     else f"All {len(output.hotspots)} hotspots owned",
            blocking=True,
        ))

        results.append(CriterionResult(
            name="Bounded contexts named",
            passed=len(output.bounded_contexts) > 0,
            evidence=f"{len(output.bounded_contexts)} context(s): {', '.join(output.bounded_contexts)}",
            blocking=True,
        ))

        results.append(CriterionResult(
            name="Success metrics quantified",
            passed=len(output.success_metrics) >= 1,
            evidence=f"{len(output.success_metrics)} metric(s) defined",
            blocking=True,
        ))

        results.append(CriterionResult(
            name="Out-of-scope exclusions documented",
            passed=len(output.out_of_scope) > 0,
            evidence=f"{len(output.out_of_scope)} exclusion(s) documented",
            blocking=False,  # warning only
        ))

        # --- AI-specific criteria ---
        results.append(CriterionResult(
            name="LLM model family specified",
            passed=output.llm_model_specified,
            evidence="LLM model documented" if output.llm_model_specified else "No model specified",
            blocking=True,
        ))

        results.append(CriterionResult(
            name="AI input constraints documented",
            passed=output.ai_constraints_documented,
            evidence="AI constraint canvas complete" if output.ai_constraints_documented
                     else "AI constraint canvas missing",
            blocking=True,
        ))

        results.append(CriterionResult(
            name="Minimum three failure modes documented",
            passed=output.failure_modes_count >= 3,
            evidence=f"{output.failure_modes_count} failure mode(s) documented",
            blocking=True,
        ))

        results.append(CriterionResult(
            name="Human-in-the-loop checkpoints enumerated",
            passed=len(output.human_checkpoints) >= 0,  # can be empty if no HITL required
            evidence=f"{len(output.human_checkpoints)} checkpoint(s) documented",
            blocking=False,
        ))

        results.append(CriterionResult(
            name="Evaluation strategy defined",
            passed=output.evaluation_strategy_defined,
            evidence="Evaluation strategy documented" if output.evaluation_strategy_defined
                     else "No evaluation strategy",
            blocking=True,
        ))

        # Determine gate result
        blocking_failures = [r for r in results if not r.passed and r.blocking]
        warning_failures = [r for r in results if not r.passed and not r.blocking]

        if blocking_failures:
            gate = GateResult.FAIL
        elif warning_failures:
            gate = GateResult.CONDITIONAL
        else:
            gate = GateResult.PASS

        return gate, results

    def report(self, output: DiscoveryOutput) -> str:
        gate, results = self.evaluate(output)
        lines = [
            f"Handoff Gate Report: {output.feature_name}",
            f"Result: {gate.value.upper()}",
            "",
            "Criteria:",
        ]
        for r in results:
            status = "PASS" if r.passed else ("BLOCK" if r.blocking else "WARN")
            lines.append(f"  [{status:<5}] {r.name}")
            lines.append(f"           {r.evidence}")
        return "\n".join(lines)

# Example: evaluating the dispute resolution feature
output = DiscoveryOutput(
    feature_name="AI Dispute Resolution Assistant",
    bounded_contexts=["Dispute Intake", "Autonomous Resolution", "Escalation"],
    hotspots=[
        {"id": "H1", "owner": "Head of Operations", "deadline": "2025-06-17"},
        {"id": "H2", "owner": None, "deadline": None},  # unowned — will fail
    ],
    ai_constraints_documented=True,
    failure_modes_count=4,
    success_metrics=["95% Tier-1 resolved without human", "<2s median response"],
    out_of_scope=["Chargebacks", "Disputes >90 days"],
    llm_model_specified=True,
    human_checkpoints=["Refunds >$200", "Fraud confidence >0.85"],
    evaluation_strategy_defined=False,  # missing — will fail
)

gate = HandoffGate()
print(gate.report(output))
A second example shows the story template generator — producing sprint-ready story text from the conversion mapping:

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal

ItemType = Literal["epic", "story", "spike", "nfr", "acceptance_criterion"]

@dataclass
class BacklogItem:
    item_type: ItemType
    title: str
    description: str
    acceptance_criteria: list[str]
    source_ref: str

def context_to_epic(context_name: str, events: list[str], aggregates: list[str]) -> BacklogItem:
    return BacklogItem(
        item_type="epic",
        title=f"{context_name} — AI Capability",
        description=(
            f"Deliver all capabilities within the {context_name} bounded context.\n"
            f"Key domain events: {', '.join(events[:3])}.\n"
            f"Key aggregates: {', '.join(aggregates)}."
        ),
        acceptance_criteria=[f"All stories in {context_name} epic are implemented and tested"],
        source_ref=f"board:context:{context_name}",
    )

def policy_to_ac(policy_text: str, context: str) -> str:
    """Convert a policy sticky to an acceptance criterion string."""
    return f"Given the system is in the {context} context, {policy_text.lower()}"

def hotspot_to_spike(hotspot_id: str, description: str, owner: str) -> BacklogItem:
    return BacklogItem(
        item_type="spike",
        title=f"Spike [{hotspot_id}]: {description[:60]}",
        description=(
            f"Investigate and resolve: {description}\n"
            f"Owner: {owner}\n"
            "Output: ADR (Architecture Decision Record) or updated policy doc."
        ),
        acceptance_criteria=[
            "Decision documented in ADR or policy doc",
            "Affected stories updated with resolution",
        ],
        source_ref=f"hotspot:{hotspot_id}",
    )

def ai_constraint_to_nfr(constraint: str, model: str) -> BacklogItem:
    return BacklogItem(
        item_type="nfr",
        title=f"NFR: {constraint[:60]}",
        description=(
            f"Non-functional requirement for the AI component (model: {model}).\n"
            f"Constraint: {constraint}"
        ),
        acceptance_criteria=[
            f"{constraint} is verified in the evaluation suite",
            "Constraint is enforced in the input validation layer",
        ],
        source_ref="ai_constraint_canvas",
    )

# Generate backlog items from the dispute resolution discovery output
epic = context_to_epic(
    "Autonomous Resolution",
    events=["Fraud pattern matched", "Refund decision generated", "Customer notified"],
    aggregates=["ResolutionDecision", "RefundAuthorisation"],
)
print(epic)

spike = hotspot_to_spike("H2", "PII masking required before sending to LLM — which fields?", "Data Engineer")
print(spike)

nfr = ai_constraint_to_nfr(
    "No raw PAN (payment card number) may be included in the LLM prompt context",
    model="claude-3.5-sonnet"
)
print(nfr)
Real-World Example

A legal technology company building an AI contract review assistant ran four discovery workshops over three weeks before attempting a backlog handoff. On the first handoff attempt, the gate evaluation revealed that seven of twelve hotspots had no assigned owners and that the AI constraint canvas had not been completed. The gate was failed, and a follow-up session was scheduled to resolve the open items.

On the second attempt, all hotspots were owned and the AI constraint canvas specified that the LLM could receive the full contract text but not the party names or signatory information (de-identified before transmission). The evaluation strategy was defined as a combination of automated LLM-as-judge evaluation against a golden dataset of 200 annotated contracts and a weekly human review of 50 randomly sampled live outputs. The gate passed, and the backlog conversion produced 14 epics, 67 stories, 9 spike tasks, and 22 acceptance criteria. The feature entered implementation with the lowest rate of mid-sprint scope changes of any AI feature the company had shipped to that point.
Common Mistakes
Making all gate criteria blocking
— not all criteria are equally critical. Categorising criteria as blocking vs. warning allows teams to proceed with minor gaps while preventing handoff with major ones.
Conflating hotspot resolution with hotspot ownership

— the gate requires hotspots to be owned, not resolved. Requiring resolution before handoff creates a chicken-and-egg problem where spikes needed to resolve hotspots cannot be scheduled because the backlog has not been created.
Skipping the readiness review for AI stories

— engineers reviewing AI feature stories before sprint planning often identify missing technical prerequisites (model API access, evaluation infrastructure, test data) that are not apparent to product management.
Treating the handoff as a one-time event
— as new hotspots are resolved and new constraints are discovered during implementation, the handoff artefacts should be updated. The gate output is a living document, not a sealed deliverable.
Not versioning the backlog conversion

— when the discovery output changes (hotspot resolved, scope narrowed), the corresponding backlog items must be updated. Without version links between backlog items and discovery artefacts, these updates are missed.
Best Practices

Run the gate evaluation immediately after the discovery session, while participants are still available to assign owners to unowned hotspots. Evaluation that happens 48 hours later faces the problem of finding people to assign.

Include the evaluation strategy criterion as blocking for all AI features. Teams that do not define how they will measure AI quality before building inevitably discover that measurement is impossible without significant architectural changes that were not planned.

Use a dedicated backlog item type for AI NFRs distinct from standard NFR stories. This allows the portfolio view to quickly identify all AI constraint requirements across features and review them collectively for consistency.

Create a "handoff audit" practice where a senior engineer reviews 20% of completed features' handoff gate outputs against what was actually built. Systematic divergence between the gate output and the implementation reveals which criteria need strengthening.

Automate what is automatable: a CI check that validates all backlog items linked to an AI feature have source discovery artefact references prevents the traceability from eroding as stories are cloned, moved, or reformatted.
Performance and Scalability

The handoff gate adds time to the discovery-to-implementation pipeline. For a typical GenAI feature, gate evaluation takes 30–60 minutes; remediation of a failed gate takes one to three additional working days. This cost is consistently recovered in reduced mid-sprint scope changes, which typically consume two to four engineering days per affected sprint.

At scale, when multiple teams run discovery workshops in parallel, a shared gate evaluation service (even a simple shared spreadsheet with a standardised format) enables portfolio-level visibility into which features are ready for implementation and which are still in discovery. This visibility is essential for sprint planning across teams that share dependencies on LLM infrastructure, evaluation tooling, or data pipelines.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Tool
Role in Handoff
Notes
Notion / Confluence
Gate checklist, session summary, conversion mapping
Use a template page with locked sections to prevent format drift across features
Jira / Linear
Receiving converted backlog items
Add a custom "discovery source" field to all item types to store traceability links
GitHub Issues / PRs
Gate checklist as PR review template
Engineering-native teams may prefer managing gate evaluation as a PR on the discovery artefact repo
Google Sheets
Hotspot owner tracking + gate metrics
Simple but effective for organisations early in the practice
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Readiness Signal
AI Support
Risk
Explicit handoff gate
Verifiable checklist
AI-specific criteria included
Low — gaps caught before implementation
Standard Agile definition of ready
Story-level criteria only
Rarely AI-adapted
Medium — AI risks not systematically captured
PM judgment call
Implicit, person-dependent
Ad hoc
High — inconsistent quality across features
No gate (immediate build)
None
None
Very high — scope ambiguity discovered mid-sprint
Security and Reliability

The handoff gate's AI constraint criterion is a critical security checkpoint. Requiring documentation of which data types may be sent to an LLM before implementation begins prevents the common pattern where developers send broader data than policy permits because no constraint was articulated. This single criterion has prevented PHI and PII leakage in organisations that implemented it retroactively after discovering uncontrolled data flows in production.

From a reliability perspective, the failure mode criterion (requiring at least three failure modes to be documented) ensures that graceful degradation paths are designed before the happy path is implemented. AI features that degrade gracefully when the LLM is unavailable, returns an unexpected format, or produces a low-confidence output are significantly more reliable in production than features built entirely around the success path.
Key Takeaways
Handoff criteria are a contract between discovery and implementation — both parties must agree on them and hold each other accountable for the gate to function as intended.
AI features require criteria beyond standard Agile definition-of-ready: model specification, input constraints, failure modes, evaluation strategy, and human checkpoint documentation.
The gate should distinguish blocking from warning criteria — blocking failures prevent handoff, warning failures allow conditional handoff with documented debt items.
Hotspot ownership at handoff time is a gate requirement; hotspot resolution is a spike task that can be tracked in the backlog post-handoff.
The backlog conversion mapping (context → epic, aggregate → story, policy → AC, hotspot → spike) ensures complete and consistent translation of discovery artefacts into implementable work items.
Gate quality should be tracked through downstream metrics — mid-sprint scope change rate and discovery re-open rate — and the criteria updated quarterly based on what patterns emerge.
